Case Study 02 · Strategic Vision · Principal-Level Work

The Orchestration
Moat.

A strategic vision for how PayPal stays the dominant force in payments. Not by defending its position, but by redefining the layer it competes on.

01
The Insight

The Org Was Asking
The Wrong Question

Inside PayPal, the question was constant: what's the difference between Braintree and PayPal? The answer was always customization. But the question kept coming back. Eventually I realized it was never really a question. It was a political position, a quiet push, years after the acquisition, to justify existing org hierarchies.

Braintree serves enterprise merchants who want full control, maximum optimization, and deep customization. PayPal serves businesses that want something ready to go: reliable, trusted, fast. These aren't competitors. They're complements.

Braintree
Built to be Customized

Full control. Deep integration. For engineers who want to optimize every layer of the payment stack.

PayPal
Built to Just Work

Out-of-the-box ready. Consumer-trusted. For businesses that want a complete solution without the complexity.

Forcing a choice between them isn't strategy. It's self-imposed market shrinkage.

02
The Problem

A Walled Garden
Nobody Asked For

PayPal's branded buttons, PayPal, Venmo, and Buy Now Pay Later, are its highest-margin revenue. And right now, they're trapped inside PayPal's own gateway.

If a merchant is already using Adyen, Stripe, Chase Payment Tech, or any other PSP, adding the PayPal button means an enormous integration cost: switching gateways, rebuilding infrastructure, internal political battles. So they don't add it. And PayPal loses its highest-margin volume not because merchants don't want it, but because we made it too hard.

Adyen
Stripe
Chase
Square
Worldpay
Checkout.com
Any PSP

↑ Merchants on these platforms can't easily add PayPal's branded buttons today

PayPal loses margin not because its products are inferior, but because its integration story requires merchants to abandon the infrastructure they've already built.

03
The Vision

Two Moves That
Change Everything

Two strategic repositionings that work together: one to make PayPal ubiquitous, one to make Braintree indispensable.

Move 1 — Decouple the Branded Button

PayPal should reframe its branded buttons not as features of its gateway, but as alternative payment methods that work on any payment stack. Just like Apple Pay and Google Pay integrate with any PSP through a standard SDK, PayPal's buttons should do the same.

  • Simple, lightweight SDK integration for any PSP
  • No requirement to switch gateways or processing infrastructure
  • Merchant keeps their existing stack. PayPal gains branded volume
  • Every new integration is a new margin-generating surface

This makes PayPal ubiquitous. A merchant on Stripe adds PayPal checkout in a day. The button follows the merchant, not the other way around.

Move 2 — Build the Orchestration Layer

Enterprise merchants almost universally use multiple PSPs simultaneously. They route transactions based on cost, geography, uptime, and conversion rate. Today, they build and maintain this logic themselves, or pay a middleware company like Primer to do it.

Primer is instructive. They do orchestration well. But they have no margin in it. No branded button. No processing. They are pure middleware, useful but not positioned to own anything.

PayPal has what Primer doesn't: a brand, a button, a processing network, and 400M+ consumer accounts. The orchestration layer is the moat, and PayPal is uniquely positioned to build it.

The proposal: Braintree operates one level above the PSP layer. Rather than competing head-to-head with Stripe and Adyen for gateway share, Braintree builds a merchant-facing orchestration platform that:

  • Routes transactions intelligently across any PSP the merchant uses
  • Surfaces performance data, analytics, and optimization insights in one place
  • Manages relationships with all third-party plugins and payment network services
  • Injects PayPal's branded buttons as a native option regardless of the underlying PSP
  • Unified servicing across all transaction origins. Disputes, reconciliation, and reporting in a single dashboard rather than scattered across multiple provider portals.
04
Architecture

The Stack
Reimagined

Three layers. Each one feeding the next. Each one reinforcing the moat.

Layer 1 — BraintreeOrchestration Platform
Routes transactions, surfaces analytics, manages PSP relationships. The merchant's command center above the stack. Once a merchant's infrastructure runs through this layer, switching costs become architectural, not contractual.
Layer 2 — PayPalBranded APMs
PayPal, Venmo, and BNPL surface as native checkout options on any payment stack: high-margin, consumer-trusted, frictionless to add. No gateway switch required.
Layer 3 — PSPsMerchant's Choice
Adyen, Stripe, Chase, Braintree direct. Merchant uses whichever they want. Braintree orchestrates above them all. Competition becomes distribution.

Competitors can win gateway share without displacing Braintree's orchestration value. Every Stripe merchant who adds the PayPal button becomes a new margin source.

05
Build vs Buy

The Primer
Question

Primer has already built much of the orchestration infrastructure this vision requires. They're technically strong and not profitable as standalone middleware.

Acquire Primer
  • Proven orchestration infrastructure from day one
  • Eliminates a potential acquisition target for Stripe or Adyen
  • Faster time to market
  • Existing merchant relationships and integrations
Build In-House
  • Full control over architecture and roadmap
  • No integration overhead or culture clash
  • Requires significant sustained investment
  • Longer path to market, higher execution risk

The recommendation: acquire Primer, build the branded APM decoupling in parallel, and position Braintree as the orchestration layer for enterprise payments within 24 months.

06
Takeaways

What This
Demonstrates

SkillDomain Expertise
Deep fluency in payments infrastructure, PSP economics, and merchant behavior. Built through years of hands-on product work inside the industry, not research reports.
SkillStrategic Framing
Identified a competitive moat before the org could see it, and articulated a clear, actionable path to owning it.
SkillCross-Functional Courage
Challenged internal political assumptions and advocated for a direction that breaks existing org boundaries, as a designer.
LevelPrincipal+ Thinking
Work at the principal designer or design director level, in both scope and strategic impact.
The best strategic moves look obvious in hindsight.
This one hadn't been made yet.
